meta.title: C207 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes C207 engine — M272, M276, OM642, OM651, M156, M278. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the pre‑2010 V6 balance‑shaft risk explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes C207 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned C207 engine typically costs £2,500–£14,000 supply only [MB‑QUOTE], depending on engine — the OM651 four‑cylinder diesel runs £2,500–£4,500, while the M157 V8 in the E 63 AMG can cost £7,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £780–£1,430. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes C207 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The post‑facelift M276 3.5‑litre V6 petrol (E 350) is the most dependable choice — it's a modern, well‑sorted engine without the balance‑shaft issues of earlier M272 units. The OM642 V6 diesel is robust but suffers from oil‑cooler seal leaks; the OM651 four‑cylinder diesel is reliable but needs chain monitoring.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes C207?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pre‑2010 M272 V6 petrols suffer balance‑shaft gear wear; OM642 V6 diesels have oil‑cooler seal leaks; OM651 diesels have a rear‑mounted timing chain that can stretch; M156 V8s (E 63) can develop cam lifter wear; and M278 V8 petrols can experience turbo coolant line failures. Regular servicing is critical across all engines.

It depends on the engine and year. Pre‑facelift (2009–2011) M272 V6 petrols have a known weakness; post‑2011 M276 V6s are much improved. The OM642 V6 diesel is robust but has a well‑documented oil‑cooler seal weakness. The OM651 needs chain monitoring. The M156 and M278 are high‑performance engines that demand meticulous maintenance.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes C207 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is an outright "avoid" — but a pre‑2010 M272 V6 without documented balance‑shaft replacement is a high‑risk purchase. Similarly, an early E 63 AMG (pre‑2011) without cam lifter history should be approached with caution. Always get a specialist pre‑purchase inspection.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my C207 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs include: cold‑start rattle (OM651 chain), burning oil smell (OM642 seals), a ticking or rattling from the front of the engine (M272 balance shaft), a top‑end tick that worsens (M156/M157 lifters), or coolant loss with no visible leak (M278 coolant lines). If you experience any of these, get a diagnosis immediately — early intervention saves thousands.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes C207 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £15,000 C207, a £5,500 OM642 recon is 37% of the car's value — safe if the car is otherwise sound. Against a tired £7,000 example, the same recon is 79% — exceeding our 70% Mercedes threshold, so evaluate scrap. Use our 70% rule as your guide.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes C207 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper maintenance, the M276 V6 petrol can exceed 150,000 miles, and the OM642 V6 diesel can reach 200,000+ miles with regular oil changes. The OM651 diesel is also capable of high mileage but requires chain monitoring. The M156/M157 V8s are durable but demand expensive preventative maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the C207 and A207?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both share the same platform and engines. The C207 is the 2‑door coupé (2009–2017, this page), while the A207 is the 2‑door convertible (2010–2017). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see the A207's own Generation Page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes C207 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen) and in the vehicle documentation. The code is stamped on the engine block — on OM651/OM642 it's on the front left, on M272/M276/M278/M156 it's on the right side. Tell us your variant and registration year, and we'll identify it for you.

hero.h1: Mercedes E-Class S212 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E-Class estate — from the frugal four-cylinder diesels to the twin-turbo V8s and the 2013 facelift's 9G-Tronic upgrades. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes E-Class S212 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E-Class estate specifically — from the pre-facelift M271/M272 units to the post-2013 M274/M276 replacements and the AMG V8s.

overview.intro: The Mercedes-Benz E-Class S212 — the estate version of the fourth-generation E-Class — was unveiled at the 2009 Frankfurt Motor Show and released for sale in Europe on 5 August 2009. Based on the W212 platform, the S212 offered a class-leading 695 litres of boot space, rising to 1,950 litres with the rear seats folded. A significant facelift in 2013 brought updated styling, new engine options, the 9G-Tronic transmission and improved efficiency. The S212 was produced until 2016, when it was replaced by the S213.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W212 (shared with saloon) • Predecessor: S211 (2003–2009) • Successor: S213 (2016–2023) • Sibling body styles in this generation: W212 Saloon, C207/A207 Coupé/Convertible.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: No [MB-VERIFIED] enquiry data exists for the E-Class S212 — the model is absent from EM_Proprietary_Data's per-model table, reflecting its lower-volume UK status compared to the W212 saloon.

items.quote: The E-Class S212's engine range spans from economical four-cylinder diesels to fire-breathing AMG V8s. For most buyers, the E 350 CDI (OM642 V6 diesel) is the standout: smooth, refined and robust with few major failure modes beyond the well-documented oil cooler seal leak. The E 250 CDI (OM651) is the sensible diesel choice for most buyers: economical, well-supported and with 204 bhp, more than adequate for the estate's size. On the petrol side, the post-2013 E 400 (M276 twin-turbo V6) offers a sweet-spot balance of performance and refinement, though early M276 examples can suffer from timing chain and cam adjuster issues. The E 500 (M278 V8) delivers effortless V8 grunt, but the hot-vee design means higher running costs and potential turbo-related issues. The E 63 AMG (M157 V8) is a genuine performance icon — 525–585 bhp from a twin-turbo 5.5-litre V8 — but running costs are substantial and the engine is complex. The pre-facelift M272 V6 (E 300) carries balance shaft sprocket wear risk; the pre-facelift M271 (E 200 CGI) has timing chain concerns. Buying instruction: prioritise post-2013 facelift cars with the 9G-Tronic for better refinement; for long-term ownership, the OM642 diesel is the most reliable choice, followed by the post-2013 M276 petrols.

rows.ownershipVerdict: V8 luxury — fast, but costly to maintain

economicsRule.title: The S212 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,500–£6,000 E 350 CDI recon against a £5,000–£15,000 S212 sits at 23–120% of the car's value. On a sub-£7,000 example, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £12,000+ example, the same recon drops to a more manageable 29–50% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S212 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 250 CDI (OM651) — simple diesel, robust, cheap parts.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S212 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 63 AMG (M157) — twin-turbo V8 complexity, AMG-specific parts, high running costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S212 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 350 CDI (OM642) — the most reliable engine in the range with strong economy and refinement.

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak
problems.rootCause: The oil cooler seals on the OM642 V6 diesel are a known weak point. The seals degrade over time, allowing oil to leak into the valley of the V6. The repair is labour-intensive, requiring removal of the intake manifold and significant disassembly.
problems.recommendation: On any E 350 CDI over 80,000 miles, budget for an oil cooler seal replacement — £800–£1,500 is a reasonable preventative cost to avoid potential engine damage from oil loss.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your S212 →

problems.title: OM651 Injector Seal Failure ("Black Death")
problems.rootCause: The injector seals on the OM651 diesel engine degrade over time, allowing combustion gases to escape past the seal. This causes a characteristic black, tar-like carbon buildup around the injector — known in Mercedes circles as "Black Death" — which can eventually seize the injector in the cylinder head.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of a diesel smell in the cabin or visible black residue around injectors, get the seals inspected immediately — a £400–£800 seal replacement is far cheaper than the £900–£1,500 head repair that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare OM651 engine prices for your S212 →

problems.title: M276 Timing Chain and Cam Adjuster Wear
problems.rootCause: The M276 V6 petrol engine family can suffer from premature timing chain stretch and wear to the camshaft adjuster sprockets. Cold-start rattling is the classic warning sign — if ignored, the chain can jump, causing piston-to-valve contact and catastrophic engine failure.
problems.recommendation: On any pre-facelift M276-engined S212 with no documented chain history, treat this as overdue preventative maintenance — an £800–£1,800 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows if ignored. Post-2013 facelift cars with the M276.820 are less prone to this issue.

cta.label: Compare M276 engine prices for your S212 →

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Sprocket Wear
problems.rootCause: Early M272 V6 engines (pre-2011) suffer from premature wear to the balance shaft sprocket, which is made of softer metal than the chain. This causes the timing chain to jump, leading to engine misfire, rough running and, in severe cases, piston-to-valve contact.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a pre-facelift E 300, check the engine number against the affected range — Mercedes revised the sprocket in 2011. A £1,200–£2,000 preventative repair is cheaper than the £2,800–£5,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M272 engine prices for your S212 →

problems.title: M278 Hot-Vee V8 Turbo Coolant Leaks
problems.rootCause: The M278 twin-turbo V8's "hot-vee" design places the turbos inside the V of the engine, generating significant heat. This causes the turbo coolant lines and valve stem seals to degrade prematurely, leading to coolant leaks and oil consumption.
problems.recommendation: On any E 500, budget for a coolant system inspection at 60,000 miles — a £1,200–£2,000 preventative repair is cheaper than the £2,800–£5,000 rebuild that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M278 engine prices for your S212 →

replacementCosts.h2: S212 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the baseline figures — the S212 shares its engine bay layout with the W212 saloon, so costs are consistent.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are S212 (E-Class estate, 2009–2016) only. The W212 saloon shares the same engines but is covered on a separate Generation Page per the one-page-per-body-code rule. The E 63 AMG S-Model (585 bhp) was introduced post-facelift with 4MATIC all-wheel drive. The E 400 replaced the E 350 post-facelift (2013).
